title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure projects and discussed ongoing road maintenance, utility work, and enforcement of sewer discharge violations. Key actions included awarding a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, approving a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to create violation language for illegal sewer discharges at $100/day fines.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. The project will likely move from 2029 to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered upcoming repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating, and pavement/manhole repairs on Schofield Avenue by HOS Construction.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant flooding required dewatering operations near Weston Avenue for approximately one week. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment and servicing mowers and tractors.

Josh reported on water service repairs, well maintenance, and sanitary sewer issues. Significant clear water infiltration was identified, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the treatment plant - indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify infiltration sources. Kennedy Park Farmers Market received positive feedback regarding parking and traffic flow.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility's total value reached nearly $34 million, an increase of 6.7% due to infrastructure additions including wells 7 and 8. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total, with 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution seeks improved transportation funding for day-to-day operations, noting that current grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded. Wisconsin was noted as relatively affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Staff sought quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael explained this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in approximately $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of violations where residents discharge sump pumps into sanitary sewers. Josh reported significant clear water infiltration is increasing treatment costs. The committee debated fine amounts, with the code allowing $100-$1,000/day. Members distinguished between unknowing versus willful violations. Motion passed to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day for violations.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029, at which time the owner will pay assessment for main extension. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's utility costs (sanitary sewer and water main) total $94,860. Steam Construction will perform the work. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA was selected at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz's $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 despite being slightly higher than MSA's $70,400, due to having full-time experienced staff rather than summer interns. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding solutions
Direct staff to create sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 in utility costs
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelinek roundabout ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs at next meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update for future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations, reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as vice chair, with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as secretary. All elections passed without opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was made with a second by Renee. Passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented on behalf of the event, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer room nights than last year. A commissioner requested better tracking of hotel room usage to help with future funding decisions. The commission approved a $5,000 grant, matching last year's amount, with a request for detailed room usage data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. This was the first time the event had been hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates visited various Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese and expressed appreciation for the community.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and established a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing technical requirements and future expansion considerations.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion included an alternate storage shed location, with commissioners suggesting placing it further north to allow for future building expansion. The landscaping plan meets current relaxed code requirements. Motion to approve passed unanimously with conditions 2-5 as listed by staff.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and drainage, and a winter operational inspection focusing on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff identified one existing facility near Sternberg that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process and is closest to the village well. Commissioners directed staff to reach out to that facility about compliance. Motion to approve passed unanimously with the addition of reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey maps/site plans and building permits for April 2026 were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan update is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion included potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, requesting staff waive excessive requirements similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested annual reporting on the salt storage shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get lost and sites are actually visited.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg about compliance with wellhead protection requirements
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invite and email regarding steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations added to future agenda for fall 2026
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received detailed overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and current initiatives.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veterans Service Office presentation by Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. She highlighted the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions, management of over 1,200 licenses for restaurants, pools, and campgrounds, and new initiatives including public health vending machines with free tick kits and a new homeless system response position. The department's 2026 budget is approximately $5 million with about half from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has approximately 140 FTE staff with a 2026 revenue budget over $22 million and tax levy of nearly $7 million. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare and youth justice populations. Leonard noted potential restructuring requests are expected due to increased needs in areas like children's long-term support and child support.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the office increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025. Customer interactions jumped from approximately 5,900 in early 2025 to 8,149 by year end. Veterans in Marathon County received $2.6 million in retroactive payments and $44 million annually in total benefits. Key initiatives include suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans), surviving spouse benefits outreach, and partnership with Center for Veterans Issues for housing stability.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He described significant financial challenges with the nutrition program, noting federal funding has remained flat while food costs increased 40-45% over four years. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024, demonstrating the regional model's cost-effectiveness.

Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness following news of a virus outbreak on a cruise ship. Health Officer Suginaka confirmed extensive plans are in place, including recent measles response training exercises. She explained staff would be reallocated from chronic disease work to emergency response if needed, and the department has a full-time public health emergency preparedness position.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)
Committee members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Strategic plan discussion to be brought to future meetings by Chair Boots

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increases to park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call, and public comments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing on Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke in public comment arguing there was no solid data justifying the specific amounts. Trustee Barb supported the fees as justified given population increases. Ordinance 26-010 passed unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule also passed unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and surrounding municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Under this system, if a food truck is inspected by any participating department, a sticker is placed on it recognizing the inspection across all communities, eliminating the need for multiple inspections. The ordinance passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the policy language about health, safety, and welfare reasons didn't match what was discussed at the previous meeting. After discussion, Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy by striking sentences requiring specific justifications for remote attendance, keeping only requirements to notify 12 hours in advance, have camera on, and prohibiting remote participation in closed sessions. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermling and Lopesell. The original motion as amended also passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golda Mua to the CLIPS committee. Daniels is currently on Finance and HR. The motion to approve both appointments passed unanimously. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: The board considered a license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar amounts. The motion was amended to require satisfactory completion of background check and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. The amended motion passed unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue site to development. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson and in the park. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate having their vehicles with them. Mock Miller Park was considered but deemed too far from traffic. The board acknowledged if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to be suspended. Renee clarified the market is not losing money and she obtains grants for programs like double-up food bucks.

During public comment, Jim Pitel criticized spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants and asked why the engineering technician position hasn't been filled. Public Works Director Michael Dolan later explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set needed is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms struggling to find local talent.

Public Works Director Dolan reported that the DOT engineering study classified Weston Avenue as a 35 mph road, not the 45 mph currently posted. He conducted a speed study showing the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, which would support keeping it at that speed. He needs to complete additional data collection once construction is finished before bringing a recommendation back to the board.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the board for spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants instead of filling the vacant engineering technician position. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting that Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before being officially appointed by the board, calling this 'inappropriate government business taking action outside of this boardroom.' He further criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel argued the fee increases lacked justification based on actual operational data.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golda Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance approved
E-bike ordinance amendments approved
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement for Ringle building inspection assistance
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1 at 6 PM
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held its adjourned organizational meeting, approving committee appointments for the new term and electing Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions. The board also unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the severe weather emergency declaration, dropping rates from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for affected residents.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, developing leadership among newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous, with some technical issues noted where Supervisor Nisa was marked absent but unable to vote.

discussions.item: Election of at-large executive committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Leur, citing her advocacy for children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two of the three nominees. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
discussions.body: Resolution from Health and Human Services and HR Finance and Property committees. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for storm debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent severe weather emergency. The state is waiving $13.10/ton and Ringle is waiving $2.33/ton. The county proposed reducing fees from the standard $65/ton gate rate to approximately $36.94/ton (covering costs only) through June 17th. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely. Multiple supervisors asked questions about coverage, timelines, and tracking. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly, then approved Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle had waived their portions and that residents are facing significant unreimbursed costs. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and requested extended fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris to approximately $36.94/ton through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with the Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanisms for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop a clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Supervisors interested in attending Solid Waste Board meetings to be notified of future meetings

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that only covers septic system reconnect fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Public hearing - Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Fee waiver consideration for April 17 tornado-impacted properties
Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
Conservation Planning and Zoning Department presentation

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley. The town board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation, and Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use. The town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Supervisor Drew questioned fairness to future disaster victims not in declared emergency areas. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about cost implications and consistency. After debate, Supervisor Sefelt moved to forward the resolution, Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended the motion to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or POWTS fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed, and the amended resolution was approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagenbusher provided an overview of landfill operations, explaining the $13.10 per ton state fee, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about weekly and discussed major capital expenses including $6 million cell construction.

County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the joint city-county department structure with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generating $3.25 million in economic impact from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt announced Lovelin is retiring June 1 after nearly 30 years of service.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board - covers only POWTS reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-damaged properties
Two rezoning petitions forwarded to county board for approval
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy development
Town of Green Valley and Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plans to reflect zoning changes if applicable

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved signage for the Plover River Water Trail kayak launches and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month. The meeting featured contentious public comment questioning the transparency of Friends of Kennedy Park fundraising, which staff and committee members defended as a long-term volunteer effort.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion occurred.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital dollars and fundraising timelines. Staff explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are targeting $117,000 for field lights and have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. The acting chair defended the Friends group, stating they are volunteers who shouldn't be accused of withholding information and plan to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines. Dan explained the mats were used to protect TC Energy pipelines during equipment access. The committee reviewed images of the construction process with no action required.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to the next meeting because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The acting chair noted her objection from a public commenter (Andy) would be recorded. No formal vote was taken to table.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting equipment access is limited and pressure washers cannot reach the trail. Committee member suggested anti-vandalism coatings or seal coats. Staff mentioned volunteer work days as a possibility, referencing how directors previously carried benches to the trail. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County presented version 7 of signage for the Plover River Water Trail. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Village of Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The Highway J launch may be closed this season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved a motion to install the signs and forward to the village board for approval. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving an email inquiry. Committee agreed to bring it back for future discussion. The Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and June 11th is the last open house for the bike and pedestrian plan.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street name inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating animals don't belong there and should use rivers and lakes instead. Jim Pensan (5002 [street name] Weston), speaking remotely under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and demanding transparency on funds raised from the glow event and lighting fundraiser. He accused the Friends of Kennedy Park of being 'frauds' for not sharing detailed financials.
actionItems: Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks launches; forwarded to village board for approval
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting when Chair Katrina can attend
Staff to investigate cleaning solutions and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail benches and garbage cans
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future committee discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler for the following Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledge work product transmittals - April budget status report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office operations
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

Discussion focused on TIF closure timeline. Staff indicated they are anticipating October/November for final closure, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the final numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk operations including elections, licensing, and tax collection. Key points: the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time employee; they handled the 2024 presidential election with just two staff members working extensive overtime; 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is processed through the clerk's office walk-ins; pet licensing was brought back in-house from third-party vendor Pet Data, saving $5,495 annually. Discussion occurred about the increase in pet licensing fees from $10 to $20 for neutered animals, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to municipalities to cover Humane Society costs. Committee also discussed the $30,000 annual cost of the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' and whether alternatives should be explored.

Pam noted that Weston's chief election inspector pay of $13/hour is on the lower end compared to other Marathon County municipalities. She indicated she will likely propose increasing it to $15/hour for the next two-year election term given the responsibilities involved.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at the new location has been going well. She has been on-site Saturdays from 6 AM for 6-7 hours helping vendors. Tuesday markets have faced some challenges with wind. The market is expanding to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to end of June. Trustee Daniels confirmed vendors he spoke with were very happy with the setup and location.
Evan announced this is his last meeting as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIF bonds ahead of October 1st due date
Recruit new committee members to fill vacancies including Evan's seat
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring decisions on both to gather more information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: The committee acknowledged reports from Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police departments. Roman reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to Chapter 50, including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet). Trustee Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance at length, with Chief Hunt strongly advocating for prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways, sidewalks, and trails due to safety concerns. Committee member Mike Compkey raised concerns about electric scooters used by children. The committee agreed to add language prohibiting emotos from roadways. Trustee Olsen moved to recommend approval with the emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion seconded by Chwanka and passed unanimously.

Trustee Olsen presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Michael from DPW suggested eliminating the even-odd system and instead signing 6-8 specific problem areas. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before plowing. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to identify specific exception areas to bring back in July.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest parking lot. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost about $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-$30,000. Chief Hunt had no safety concerns. The committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for requested crosswalks. Trustee Olsen moved that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost, the committee recommends board approval. Motion seconded by Chwanka and passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented a regional fire inspection program for food trucks involving Marshfield, Stevens Point, Wisconsin Rapids, Plover, Rib Mountain, and potentially Weston. One inspection would be valid across all participating municipalities. Chief noted a recent inspection found a food truck with unsafe galvanized LP piping and improperly secured propane cylinders. Chwanka moved to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), 3913 Ross Avenue (sequence correction and secondary unit), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt mentioned an additional addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski with eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze order proceedings on an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue, abandoned since approximately 2010-2014. A previous fire department training burn was deemed unsafe due to the structure's condition. The property owners are current on taxes and have agreed to the demolition approach, with costs to be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and questioning whether parking on grass violates village code. He raised concerns about ADA accessible parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt, noting difficulties for wheelchair users on wet sandy soil. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks and raised concerns about mobile food vendor regulations on public property. He also criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, suggesting the committee should receive progress updates on long-standing violations.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael from DPW to identify 6-8 specific areas for targeted parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding willingness to pay for crosswalk installation
Recommend Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to Board of Trustees
Recommend address corrections for E911 compliance to Board of Trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings on 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Public Works Committee
overview: Based on the published agenda, this meeting was scheduled to address a public hearing on special assessments for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, along with multiple infrastructure resolutions and project updates. The meeting was significant as it involved property owner assessments for driveway approach construction related to a new roundabout installation.

agenda.item: Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project Special Assessments Public Hearing

Approval of 5/28/26 Bloedel Ave Special Assessment Hearing Minutes

discussions.item: Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project Special Assessments Public Hearing
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to conduct a public hearing on special assessments for driveway approach construction related to a project replacing the existing 4-way stop intersection with a compact roundabout. Property owners were expected to have opportunity for testimony and questions, with assessments calculated at $8.61 per square foot for concrete driveway aprons and prorated for driveways installed within the past 20 years.

The committee was set to consider approval of the 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report for Weston Sewer Utility for submission to the Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources. This is a required annual regulatory compliance report.

The committee was expected to consider an authorizing resolution for the reconstruction of Bloedel Ave from Pine Park St to Alderson St. This follows a special assessment hearing held on May 28, 2026.

discussions.item: Resolution 2026-018 – Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Intersection Project
discussions.body: The committee was scheduled to consider authorization for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project, which involves replacing the 4-way stop with a roundabout, along with water main relocation, sanitary sewer improvements, and storm sewer replacement.
The committee was set to review quotes for topsoil screening services. The agenda did not provide details on the scope or vendors submitting quotes.
The committee was scheduled to review paving quotes for Shirley Ave. Specific details on project scope were not included in the agenda.

discussions.item: Contract Amendment No. 1 with MSA for Right of Way Services
discussions.body: The committee was expected to consider a contract amendment with MSA for right of way services related to the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project.

discussions.item: Change Order No. 1 for Alderson St and Jelinek Ave Project
discussions.body: The committee was set to review the first change order for the Alderson St and Jelinek Ave intersection project. Details on the nature of the change order were not specified in the agenda.

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to hold its regular meeting on June 8, 2026, with significant financial matters including the preliminary 2026-27 budget and a debt defeasance resolution for 2022 bonds. The board was also expected to consider multiple policy updates, employee handbook changes, meal price adjustments, and a facilities project at Riverview, along with receiving updates on summer learning programs and changes to the DPI Community Eligibility Provision program.

discussions.body: Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting and was expected to come before the board for action.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in approximately 2 minutes. The presentation was expected to include a marked-up version showing suggested edits and a clean version with changes accepted for board review and approval.

Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in approximately 2 minutes. The item was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting and included supporting documentation including a PLE Tool analysis.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously reviewed at the May Committee Meeting and was expected to be considered for board action.

discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in approximately 2 minutes. The policy revision was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 concerning the High School Activities Code in approximately 2 minutes. This item was previously reviewed at the May Committee Meeting.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project for RFP approval in approximately 2 minutes. The project was previously presented at the May Committee Meeting.

The presentation was expected to seek board adoption of a resolution authorizing the transfer of funds, establishment of an escrow account, and defeasance of certain General Obligation School Building and Facility Improvement Bonds, Series 2022, with official defeasance language provided by Quarles & Brady.

Dr. Rob Phelps and Mrs. Vickie Christianson were scheduled to give a brief 5-minute summary of the Summer Learning Program in the Wausau School District. This was an informational presentation with no action requested.

Tiffany Miskowski was scheduled to share a brief 5-minute update on recent changes to the DPI Community Eligibility Provision (CEP) program. This was an informational item regarding state-level program changes.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
